entries.legalName: Digital ID Scheme (announced under Immigration and Asylum Act 2025 provisions)

severity.rationale: Universal application for employment [+1], criminal penalties for non-compliance [+1], biometric collection mandated [+1], no judicial oversight for data sharing [+1], mission creep to housing and benefits explicit [+1], digital exclusion of undocumented migrants [+1]. Total: 6/7 → 5/5

entries.description: Mandatory digital ID cards required for all employment right-to-work checks by 2029, explicitly targeting undocumented workers and creating a two-tier labor market where migrant labor is hyper-surveilled while employers gain enhanced verification powers.

sources.description: UK Government press release announcing mandatory digital ID for right-to-work checks
Big Brother Watch opposition analysis citing 'domestic mass surveillance infrastructure'
Institute for Government analysis of labor market enforcement implications

entries.notes: Faced massive opposition including 2.9 million signature petition; Labour government backed down on 'compulsory' language January 2026 but maintained mandatory requirements for employment. Opposition spans Corbyn-aligned Labour MPs, SNP, Lib Dems, and Northern Ireland parties. Compared to Blair's failed ID scheme but with enhanced biometric capabilities. Contractors likely include Oracle and Yoti. Creates severe chilling effect for union organizing among undocumented workers who will be excluded from formal employment entirely.

entries.commonName: Online Safety Act (Age Verification and Encryption)
entries.legalName: Online Safety Act 2023 (as amended)

severity.rationale: Universal application to all internet users [+1], criminal penalties for platforms (10% global revenue) [+1], irreversible biometric age estimation data collection [+1], chilling effect on organizing by requiring identity for social media [+1], mission creep from pornography to all 'harmful content' [+1], no judicial oversight for Ofcom notices [+1], digital exclusion for those without ID [+1]. Total: 7/7 → 5/5

entries.description: Requires 'highly effective' age verification for adult content platforms by July 2025, with provisions enabling Ofcom to mandate client-side scanning of encrypted messages, effectively outlawing end-to-end encryption while creating mandatory identity verification for internet access.

sources.description: Ofcom official guidance on age verification requirements effective July 2025
Open Rights Group briefing on encryption dismantling and free speech risks
Conservative analysis highlighting Section 122 client-side scanning powers

entries.notes: Active enforcement from July 25, 2025. Forces platforms to implement facial age estimation, government ID submission, or digital identity wallets. Section 121 grants Ofcom power to require 'accredited technology' (client-side scanning) for encrypted messaging. WhatsApp, Signal, Element threatened UK withdrawal. Apple removed Advanced Data Protection from UK market rather than comply with backdoor demands. Worker organizing communications vulnerable to employer-mandated surveillance under 'duties to prevent harmful content.'

entries.legalName: Investigatory Powers (Amendment) Act 2024

severity.rationale: Universal data retention applies to entire population [+1], no criminal penalties for individuals but severe surveillance [+0], irreversible bulk personal dataset collection [+1], explicit targeting of journalists and organizers possible [+1], mission creep to new agencies (IPO, ambulance services) [+1], limited judicial oversight via 'double lock' but broad ministerial discretion [+0.5], no digital exclusion [+0]. Total: 4.5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: Expands bulk personal dataset retention and adds new public authorities (including Intellectual Property Office and ambulance services) authorized to access communications data without warrant, with implementation commencing April-June 2025.

sources.description: Official text of Investigatory Powers (Amendment) Act 2024
Parliamentary debate on implementation regulations March 2025
Civil liberties monitoring of expanded authority scope

entries.notes: Commenced April 25, 2025 for third-party bulk datasets; June 20, 2025 for communications data authorities. Expands warrantless access to subscriber information for copyright enforcement (IPO) and ambulance services. 'Double lock' authorization claims judicial oversight but Judicial Commissioners are former judges appointed by government. Union communications particularly vulnerable as 'economic well-being' justifies surveillance. Links to Renters' Rights Act 2025 investigatory powers for landlord enforcement.

entries.legalName: BND-Gesetz Reform 2025 (draft)

severity.rationale: Universal application - traffic retention at internet nodes captures entire population [+1], criminal penalties for non-compliance by providers [+1], irreversible full-content interception and 6-month data retention [+1], explicit targeting of journalists and foreign nationals in Germany [+1], mission creep to 'offensive hacking' of foreign providers [+1], no judicial oversight for foreign intelligence collection [+1], creates digital exclusion by surveillance chilling effect [+0.5]. Total: 6.5/7 → 5/5

entries.description: Comprehensive expansion of Federal Intelligence Service (BND) powers including full-content interception at internet exchange nodes, 6-month data retention, offensive hacking of foreign technology providers, and surveillance of foreign journalists in Germany.

sources.description: SC Media report on 139-page draft legislation doubling BND remit
Netzpolitik.org analysis (typical critical coverage - specific article pending publication)

entries.notes: Draft legislation aims to reduce reliance on US intelligence by expanding German mass surveillance capabilities to match UK GCHQ and French DGSE. 'Hack foreign providers' clause creates friction with US tech platforms (Google, Meta, X). Removes distinction between intelligence and law enforcement by allowing 'federal trojan' use. Part of post-war privacy erosion despite constitutional protections. History of data retention laws being struck down by Constitutional Court suggests this will face legal challenge if passed.

entries.legalName: Neue Personalausweis (nPA) Digital Wallet Expansion

severity.rationale: Universal assignment of digital identity to all citizens [+1], no criminal penalties yet but administrative penalties likely [+0.5], biometric collection (facial recognition for smartphone storage) [+1], potential chilling effect if linked to employment [+0.5], explicit mission creep to EU Digital Identity Wallet framework [+1], judicial oversight for data access unclear [+0.5], digital exclusion of those without smartphones [+1]. Total: 4.5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: Nationwide rollout of smartphone-based digital ID cards allowing biometric verification for employment, banking, and healthcare, integrated with EU Digital Identity Wallet framework requiring implementation by 2027.

sources.description: Technical overview of April 2025 nationwide launch
Federal Ministry of Interior official digital identity strategy

entries.notes: Voluntary in theory but 'every citizen will be assigned a digital identity.' Coalition government claims alignment with EU eIDAS regulation. Involves Google and Samsung in wallet development. Connects to BundID and BundesIdent previous systems criticized for low adoption due to complexity. Explicitly designed to enable employment verification, raising concerns about eventual mandatory use for work following UK model. EU interoperability means data sharing across member states with varying privacy protections.

entries.legalName: Verordnung zur Verhinderung der Verbreitung sexuellen Missbrauchs von Kindern (CSAR)

severity.rationale: Universal application to all EU messaging users [+1], criminal penalties for non-compliant platforms [+1], irreversible client-side scanning creates permanent surveillance infrastructure [+1], severe chilling effect on worker organizing and confidential communications [+1], mission creep from CSAM to 'unknown content' scanning explicit [+1], no judicial oversight for AI-based detection [+1], creates digital exclusion by banning anonymous communication [+1]. Total: 7/7 → 5/5 (proposed as 4 due to ongoing opposition)

entries.description: German government split on supporting EU-wide 'Chat Control' regulation mandating client-side scanning of encrypted messages for CSAM detection, with current draft including mandatory age verification and voluntary but incentivized mass scanning.

sources.description: POLITICO reporting on German government split November 2025
Tuta (encrypted email) civil liberties analysis of Danish presidency proposal
MEP Patrick Breyer's detailed legal analysis of CSAR proposal

entries.notes: Justice Minister Hubig opposes mass scanning but Interior Ministry leads negotiations and may support watered-down version. November 2025 Council position removed mandatory scanning but retained voluntary scanning incentives and mandatory age verification. Historical parallel to East German Stasi surveillance creates unique political sensitivity. If passed, would require scanning of WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage. German Constitutional Court previously struck down data retention; similar challenge likely. Worker communications currently protected by strong labor privacy laws would be exposed to employer-accessible platform surveillance.

entries.commonName: State Social Media Age Verification Laws
entries.legalName: Various state laws (Texas HB 18, Louisiana Act 456/481, California SB 976, etc.)

severity.rationale: Universal application to all social media users in affected states [+1], criminal/civil penalties for platforms [+1], biometric and government ID data collection [+1], chilling effect on youth organizing and political speech [+1], mission creep from 'protecting minors' to general identity verification [+1], no judicial oversight for data sharing with law enforcement [+0.5], digital exclusion for unbanked/undocumented [+1]. Total: 5.5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: Patchwork of state laws mandating government ID or biometric age verification for social media access, with Texas, Louisiana, and others actively enforcing while courts issue preliminary injunctions in Arkansas, Ohio, and California.

sources.description: Legal analysis of state patchwork including Texas injunction
MultiState tracking of state legislative enactments
Comprehensive civil liberties tracking of state laws

entries.notes: 50-state patchwork creates compliance nightmare. Texas App Store Accountability Act enjoined December 2025; Louisiana Act 456 permanently enjoined December 2025 but Act 481 remains effective July 2026. NetChoice (industry group) challenging on First Amendment grounds with mixed success. Creates dual-class internet: verified (ID holders) and unverified (excluded). Disproportionately impacts undocumented workers, youth organizers, and low-income communities without government ID. Data likely shared with ICE/DHS given lack of privacy protections in most state laws.

entries.legalName: Eliminating Abusive and Rampant Neglect of Interactive Technologies Act

severity.rationale: Universal application to all encrypted communications [+1], criminal liability for platform employees (Section 230 carveout) [+1], irreversible mandate for client-side scanning or backdoors [+1], severe chilling effect on organizing (workers cannot communicate securely) [+1], mission creep from CSAM to all 'illegal content' [+1], no judicial oversight for scanning mandates [+1], creates digital exclusion by making encrypted services unavailable [+1]. Total: 7/7 → 5/5

entries.description: Federal legislation threatening Section 230 liability protections for platforms offering end-to-end encryption unless they implement client-side scanning, effectively mandating encryption backdoors under the guise of combating CSAM.

sources.description: Congress.gov bill tracking (reintroduced 2023-2024 session)
Stanford Cyberlaw Center analysis of encryption threats
Internet Society technical impact assessment

entries.notes: Did not advance to full vote in 2023-2024 session but provisions reintroduced in subsequent sessions. Would force platforms to choose: abandon encryption or face state-level civil liability. Mozilla, EFF, Signal, WhatsApp opposed. Worker organizing particularly threatened as E2EE currently protects union communications from employer surveillance. If passed, would force labor organizers to use unencrypted channels or foreign platforms.

entries.legalName: ICE National Criminal Analysis and Targeting Center (NCATC) Expansion

severity.rationale: Targets specific population (immigrants/visa holders) but affects entire population through chilling effect [+0.5], no criminal penalties for surveilled but detention/deportation [+1], irreversible data collection and retention [+1], explicit targeting of worker organizing among immigrant communities [+1], mission creep from 'national security' to general law enforcement [+1], no judicial oversight for open-source surveillance [+1], digital exclusion through fear of monitoring [+0.5]. Total: 5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: ICE expansion of social media monitoring through contractor-operated 'intelligence fusion centers' in Vermont and California, specifically targeting immigrant workers for deportation enforcement using commercial databases and open-source intelligence.

sources.description: VTDigger investigation of ICE contractor expansion October 2025
Wired investigative reporting on NCATC surveillance capabilities

entries.notes: Active procurement as of October 2025. 'Limited success' with current open-source monitoring driving demand for enhanced contractor capabilities. Targets Facebook, Instagram, X, and commercial databases. Explicitly generates leads for 'Worksite Enforcement Operations' targeting undocumented labor. Creates severe chilling effect on immigrant worker organizing - workers afraid to post about wage theft or unsafe conditions. Part of broader DHS 'border security' privatization. Contractors likely include Palantir and similar surveillance tech firms.

entries.legalName: Amazon Labor Tracking System (internal)

severity.rationale: Applies to specific workforce (warehouse/gig workers) not entire population [+0], no criminal penalties but termination/blacklisting [+0.5], biometric badge data collection potentially permanent [+1], explicit chilling effect on union organizing [+1], mission creep to 'productivity optimization' [+0.5], no judicial oversight for workplace surveillance [+1], digital exclusion through algorithmic management [+0.5]. Total: 4.5/7 → 3/5

entries.description: Systematic surveillance of warehouse workers through barcode badge tracking, 'scan to scan' productivity metrics, and algorithmic management systems that automatically terminate workers failing to meet quotas, explicitly used to prevent union formation.

sources.description: Academic research: 'Fulfillment of the Work Games: Warehouse Workers' Experiences with Algorithmic Management'
OSHA documentation of workplace surveillance (contextual)

entries.notes: Active implementation across Amazon fulfillment network. Barcode badges track location, productivity, and 'time off task.' Algorithmic termination systems operate without human review. Research shows workers understand badges as identity and surveillance mechanism: 'Your badge is everything... that's how they track you.' Creates panopticon effect preventing union organizing. Similar systems deployed at UPS, FedEx, and gig platforms (Uber, DoorDash). No federal legislation protecting workers from algorithmic management. NLRB has challenged some implementations but protections weak.

entries.commonName: Bill C-2 (Strong Borders Act) - Lawful Access
entries.legalName: Bill C-2, An Act respecting certain measures relating to the security of the border

severity.rationale: Universal application to 'any person providing services to the public' [+1], criminal penalties for non-compliance (fines up to $5,000, potential imprisonment) [+1], irreversible subscriber data and IP retention [+1], chilling effect on journalists and organizers [+1], mission creep to medical records and Canada Post mail [+1], warrantless access removes judicial oversight [+1], digital exclusion through fear of surveillance [+0.5]. Total: 5.5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: Omnibus border security bill attempting to introduce warrantless access to subscriber data and IP addresses, forcing technical assistance from service providers, and expanding surveillance powers to CSIS and law enforcement under 'lawful access' framework.

sources.description: Parliament of Canada official bill text
Michael Geist (U Ottawa) analysis of withdrawn provisions and privacy implications
ABlawg constitutional analysis of Charter violations

entries.notes: Effectively withdrawn/replaced by Bill C-12 in October 2025 after massive opposition from OpenMedia, BCCLA, CIPPIC, and privacy experts. Would have allowed warrantless access to medical records, hotel bookings, and electronic communications. CSIS internal emails celebrated provisions as 'GREAT NEWS!!' Michael Geist called it 'unprecedented violation of Canadian privacy rights.' Would have enabled CLOUD Act compliance with US, exposing Canadian data to US law enforcement.

entries.legalName: Bill C-63, Online Harms Act

severity.rationale: Universal application to online platforms [+1], no criminal penalties for users but platform liability [+0.5], age verification data collection potentially permanent [+0.5], chilling effect on speech through 'duty to protect children' [+0.5], mission creep to 'harmful content' expansion likely [+0.5], Digital Safety Commission oversight (not judicial) [+0.5], digital exclusion through age verification barriers [+0.5]. Total: 4/7 → 3/5

entries.description: Proposed legislation establishing Digital Safety Commission with powers to mandate age verification and platform scanning for child protection, reintroduced after dying on order paper with 2024 prorogation.

sources.description: Department of Justice Charter Statement for Bill C-63
Osler Hoskin analysis of age verification requirements and gaps
UNICEF Canada policy brief on child rights implications

entries.notes: Died on order paper 2024; reintroduced in modified form 2025 as Bill C-216 (private member's bill) and government signaling 'fresh look.' Controversial provisions include 'digital safety duty' requiring platforms to restrict content harmful to children, which may mandate age verification. Canadian Centre for Child Protection advocating for explicit age assurance requirements. Unlike UK Online Safety Act, C-63 did not explicitly mandate encryption breaking, but left regulatory authority to require 'design features' that could include client-side scanning.

entries.commonName: Digital ID and Authentication Council of Canada (DIACC) Expansion
entries.legalName: Pan-Canadian Trust Framework (PCTF) Verified Person Component

severity.rationale: Voluntary currently but increasing pressure for adoption [+0.5], no criminal penalties [+0], biometric collection for higher assurance levels [+1], potential chilling effect on employment if adopted by gig platforms [+0.5], mission creep to banking and legal services explicit [+1], oversight by DIACC (industry body, not judicial) [+0.5], digital exclusion of those without bank accounts [+1]. Total: 4.5/7 → 3/5

entries.description: Expansion of Verified.Me and DIACC Pan-Canadian Trust Framework enabling private sector digital identity verification for legal, financial, and employment sectors, with federated but increasingly centralized biometric collection.

sources.description: DIACC official certification announcements October 2025
CIPPIC (Canadian Internet Policy and Public Interest Clinic) monitoring

entries.notes: PCTF certification achieving uptake in legal sector (FCT Client ID Verification certified October 2025). Claims 'voluntary' and 'decentralized' but architecture enables mission creep toward mandatory employment verification. No federal legislation establishing framework - operates as industry self-regulation with government participation. Concerns about 'function creep' similar to UK Digital ID. British Columbia, Ontario, and Quebec provincial digital IDs being integrated. Worker surveillance risk as gig platforms (Uber, DoorDash) adopt for 'verification' creating permanent biometric employment records without labor protections.

entries.legalName: Digital ID Act 2024 and Trust Exchange (TEx)

severity.rationale: Universal application to government services, expanding to private sector [+1], administrative penalties for non-compliance by providers [+0.5], biometric collection (facial verification) [+1], chilling effect on organizing if required for employment [+0.5], explicit mission creep to private sector banking/telco by 2026-2027 [+1], oversight by ACCC and Services Australia (not judicial) [+0.5], digital exclusion for those without smartphones/bank accounts [+1]. Total: 5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: Legislation expanding myGovID from 130 government services to private sector (banks, telcos, utilities) by 2026, with biometric facial verification and 'Trust Exchange' enabling cross-sector data sharing.

sources.description: Australian Government Digital ID System official site
Ashurst legal analysis of four-phase expansion including private sector
Critical analysis of refusal implications and privacy risks

entries.notes: Commenced December 2024; Phase 3 (private sector) allows banks, telcos, utilities to use myGovID by 2026-2027. Phase 4 allows private sector IDs for government services. Claims 'voluntary' but alternative pathways being systematically defunded. 'Trust Exchange' (TEx) enables credential sharing across government wallets. Senate amendments tightened liability but expanded ACCC oversight rather than judicial. Biometric data stored by accredited providers (including private sector) under TDIF standards. Union concerns about employer coercion to use for employment verification despite 'voluntary' claims.

entries.legalName: Online Safety Amendment (Social Media Minimum Age) Act 2024

severity.rationale: Universal age verification required for all social media users [+1], criminal penalties for platforms ($49.5M AUD fines) [+1], biometric age estimation or government ID collection [+1], severe chilling effect on youth organizing and political expression [+1], mission creep to other platforms explicit in legislation [+1], eSafety Commissioner discretion (not judicial) [+0.5], digital exclusion of under-16s and those without ID [+1]. Total: 5.5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: World-first ban on social media accounts for under-16s effective December 10, 2025, requiring platforms to implement 'reasonable steps' age verification including biometric estimation or government ID submission.

sources.description: Department of Infrastructure official implementation guidance
eSafety Commissioner regulatory guidance September 2025
University of Sydney analysis of global implications

entries.notes: Active enforcement from December 10, 2025. Platforms mass-deleting under-16 accounts. Exemptions for messaging apps, gaming, and educational services (subject to change). Penalties up to $49.5M AUD for non-compliance. 'Reasonable steps' includes facial age estimation creating biometric databases of all users (not just under-16s). No penalties for children/parents but creates surveillance infrastructure. Digital Rights Watch and EFA opposed as 'treating internet as cigarettes.' Sets global precedent being watched by UK, US states. Creates 'digital exodus' of youth to unregulated platforms. Union concerns about inability to organize young workers via social media.

entries.legalName: Telecommunications and Other Legislation Amendment (Assistance and Access) Act 2018

severity.rationale: Universal application to encrypted communications [+1], criminal penalties for providers (failure to comply) [+1], forced decryption capabilities [+1], severe chilling effect on journalist and worker communications [+1], mission creep to 'serious crime' from terrorism [+1], limited judicial oversight (AG approval only) [+0.5], no digital exclusion but creates security exclusion [+0]. Total: 5.5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: Active legislation compelling telecommunications providers and tech companies to provide technical assistance to law enforcement including decryption of encrypted communications, with Technical Capability Notices requiring systemic weaknesses.

sources.description: Department of Home Affairs official explanations (defensive)
Technical analysis of TARs, TANs, and TCNs surveillance mechanisms
Digital Rights Watch opposition campaign and analysis

entries.notes: Active since 2018 with increased use reported 2024-2025. Section 317ZG prohibits 'systemic weaknesses' but technical experts agree compelled assistance creates de facto backdoors. ASIO reports 'fewer than 20' TARs issued, but secrecy provisions prevent accurate accounting. Five Eyes outlier - only Australia lacks judicial authorization for these powers. Used for 'serious crime' not just terrorism. Threatens global security by undermining encryption standards. Worker organizing vulnerable as secure communications compromised.

entries.legalName: Kids Online Safety Act (S. 1748) / KIDS Act provisions

severity.rationale: Universal application to minors but affects all users through platform over-compliance [+1], no direct criminal penalties for users but FTC enforcement creates liability [+0.5], irreversible age verification data collection incentivized [+1], severe chilling effect on youth organizing and LGBTQ+ worker communities [+1], mission creep from 'harms' to broad content filtering evident [+1], FTC discretion without judicial oversight [+0.5], digital exclusion of youth from organizing spaces [+1]. Total: 5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: Federal legislation allowing FTC to sue platforms failing to 'prevent harms' to minors, creating incentives for invasive age verification and content filtering that disproportionately targets LGBTQ+ youth and young worker organizers while undermining Section 230 protections.

sources.description: Congress.gov bill text (2024 version, basis for 2025 reintroduction)
EFF analysis of censorship risks and age verification concerns
Fight for the Future opposition statement on federal preemption

entries.notes: Passed Senate July 2024 (91-3) but stalled in House; reintroduced 2025 as S. 1748 and incorporated into broader KIDS Act package. March 2026 House Energy & Commerce Committee hearings show continued bipartisan support but Democratic opposition over lack of 'duty of care' and state preemption. While 2025 version removed explicit 'duty of care' language, creates liability for 'serious emotional disturbance' and 'anxiety' that platforms will interpret through over-filtering. Heritage Foundation and Project 2025 allies support bill; incoming FTC Chair Andrew Ferguson (Trump appointee) has vowed to 'fight back against the trans agenda,' creating acute risk of weaponization against LGBTQ+ worker organizing content. 90+ civil liberties organizations oppose.

entries.commonName: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (formerly DPDI Bill)
entries.legalName: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (c. xx)

severity.rationale: Universal application to all data subjects [+1], administrative penalties for non-compliance [+0.5], enables permanent algorithmic decision databases [+1], explicit chilling effect on worker organizing through weakened ADM protections [+1], mission creep to AI training and 'recognized legitimate interests' [+1], removed judicial oversight for many data processing decisions [+0.5], enables digital exclusion through automated decision-making [+0.5]. Total: 4.5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: Post-Brexit data protection reform weakening UK GDPR protections to enable algorithmic management of workers, reducing automated decision-making safeguards and removing 'balancing tests' for data processing while maintaining EU adequacy status through 2025.

sources.description: Legislation.gov.uk official text (Royal Assent 19 June 2025)
Heinrich Böll Stiftung analysis of automated decision-making weakening
Lawyers and academics warning on worker data rights weakening

entries.notes: Royal Assent granted 19 June 2025; provisions phasing in through secondary legislation over 12 months. Replaces abandoned DPDI Bill with reduced but still significant weakening of data rights. Key change: Automated decision-making protections now limited to 'special category data' (race, health, etc.) only, allowing AI management tools to fire gig workers or schedule shifts without human review if based on 'performance data.' Creates 'recognized legitimate interests' bypassing balancing tests for data processing. EU adequacy status extended to December 2025 pending review of these changes. Labour government claims 'pro-innovation' approach but removes Article 22 GDPR protections that prevented solely automated decisions with legal/significant effects. Union concerns (TUC) about algorithmic management in warehouses, ride-share, and platform work largely ignored.

entries.commonName: Critical Cyber Systems Protection Act (Bill C-8)
entries.legalName: An Act respecting cyber security, amending the Telecommunications Act (Bill C-8)

severity.rationale: Universal application to telecom users and critical infrastructure [+1], criminal and administrative penalties ($15M/day for corporations) [+1], irreversible CSE data retention and Five Eyes sharing [+1], explicit targeting of infrastructure workers and potential chilling effect on strikes [+1], mission creep from cyber security to intelligence collection explicit [+1], warrantless Minister powers remove judicial oversight [+1], creates digital exclusion through surveillance infrastructure [+0.5]. Total: 5.5/7 → 5/5

entries.description: Reintroduced cybersecurity legislation granting Communications Security Establishment (CSE) and ministers warrantless powers to compel telecom data, access critical infrastructure systems, and issue secret 'cyber security directions' with potential for encryption backdoors and Five Eyes intelligence sharing.

sources.description: Parliament of Canada Bill C-8 first reading text (June 18, 2025)
Citizen Lab security analysis on encryption backdoor risks
Balsillie School of International Affairs surveillance analysis

entries.notes: Reintroduced June 18, 2025 as Bill C-8 after predecessor Bill C-26 died on order paper due to 2025 prorogation. Near-identical to C-26 but removes Canada Evidence Act amendments and adds limited appeal mechanisms. Part I (Telecommunications Act): Minister of Industry can compel 'any information' from telecoms without warrant and share with CSE/CSIS. Citizen Lab warns sections 15.2(2)(c), (l), (m) could force telecoms to 'install backdoors inside Canada's networks by weakening encryption.' Part II (CCSPA): Designated operators (energy, transport, finance, telecom) must report incidents within 72 hours to CSE and comply with secret 'cyber security directions' that cannot be disclosed publicly. CSE can repurpose cyber security data for foreign intelligence collection. Information shared with Five Eyes partners (US, UK, AU, NZ) without additional safeguards. Privacy Commissioner warned of 'warrantless search powers' and 'far-reaching information-sharing without individuals' awareness.' Intelligence Commissioner Simon Noël testified that powers bypass CSE Act oversight mechanisms. Infrastructure unions (energy, telecom, transport) subject to surveillance during job actions; no exemption for labor disputes. EU data adequacy status at risk due to surveillance provisions.

entries.legalName: SB 947 - Employment: Automated Decision Systems

severity.rationale: Applies to employers using ADS, not universal population [+0], no surveillance created but restricts existing [+0], prevents irreversible algorithmic discrimination [+0], explicitly PROTECTS worker organizing by preventing predictive retaliation [+0], prevents mission creep of worker surveillance [+0], establishes judicial oversight via private right of action [+0], reduces digital exclusion by requiring human review [+0]. Total: 0/7 → 2/5 (Low severity as protective legislation, included to show counter-surveillance efforts)

entries.description: California legislation establishing worker protections against algorithmic management by prohibiting solely automated firing/discipline decisions, banning predictive behavior analysis, and requiring human oversight with transparency for ADS use in employment.

sources.description: Official California Legislature bill text SB 947
Legal analysis of human review requirements and predictive behavior prohibitions
UC Berkeley Labor Center analysis of algorithmic management regulations

entries.notes: Reintroduced February 2026 after Governor Newsom vetoed SB 7 in October 2025. Represents legislative resistance to worker surveillance. Key protections: prohibits 'predictive behavior analysis' that profiles workers for organizing activity, requires human investigation before discipline/termination, mandates post-use notice and data access rights. California Federation of Labor Unions (AFL-CIO) sponsoring. Creates private right of action for workers. Directly counteracts Amazon-style algorithmic firing and 'robo-rating' systems in gig economy. If enacted, would be strongest state-level protection against algorithmic management in US. Faces federal preemption risk under Trump administration threats to state AI regulation.

entries.commonName: NSW Digital White Card (Construction Worker ID)
entries.legalName: Service NSW Digital White Card Program (Work Health and Safety Regulation 2017 amendments)

severity.rationale: Universal for construction workers (~600,000 in NSW) [+0.5], administrative penalties for non-compliance [+0.5], permanent digital record via Service NSW app [+0.5], potential chilling effect on undocumented construction workers [+0.5], mission creep to other trade licenses explicit [+1], no judicial oversight for data access [+0.5], digital exclusion requires smartphone/MyServiceNSW Account [+1]. Total: 4/7 → 3/5

entries.description: Mandatory digitization of construction industry safety credentials requiring workers to use Service NSW app with MyServiceNSW Account, creating biometric-linked digital trails for site access while employers verify via QR code scanning.

sources.description: Service NSW official Digital White Card terms and requirements
SafeWork NSW announcement of digital trade license program
Analysis of terms requiring charged phone, data sharing, and exclusion risks

entries.notes: Active since February 2021 but expanded 2025-2026 as 'first Digital Trade Licence' with digitization of all trade licenses planned. Construction workers (laborers, tradespeople, supervisors) must maintain smartphone with charged battery and readable screen or face exclusion from sites. QR code verification allows employers to track site entry/exit. Linked to MyServiceNSW Account which requires 100 points of ID and biometric verification. Creates two-tier system: digital card holders vs. those unable/unwilling to use smartphones. Physical card still 'mandatory backup' but digital preferred. Terms require acceptance of broad data sharing. Connected to broader 'Digital White Card' national conversation but NSW first jurisdiction. Construction unions (CFMEU) concerned about employer surveillance via verification tracking but also recognize safety compliance benefits. Undocumented workers face barriers as digital system more traceable than physical cards.

entries.legalName: Act to Combat Right-Wing Extremism and Hate Crime (NetzDG Amendment)

severity.rationale: Universal application to social media users [+1], platforms face €50M fines driving over-compliance [+0.5], irreversible police database entries for reported content [+1], severe chilling effect on political organizing and union activity [+1], mission creep from hate speech to 'economic sabotage' and protest organizing [+1], no judicial oversight before BKA referral [+1], digital exclusion through platform fear [+0.5]. Total: 5/7 → 4/5

entries.description: Amendment to Network Enforcement Act requiring social media platforms to proactively report 'manifestly criminal content' to Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA) Central Reporting Office (ZMI), creating direct pipeline from platform moderation to federal criminal investigations.

sources.description: Liber-Net analysis of ZMI BKA surveillance implications and chilling effects
Max Planck Institute human rights analysis of NetzDG surveillance effects
Federal Office of Justice official NetzDG guidance and BKA reporting requirements

entries.notes: Active since February 2022. ZMI (Zentralstelle zur Aufnahme von Strafinhalten im Internet) processes ~53,800 notices (2021-2025), projecting 250,000 annual submissions and 150,000 criminal proceedings. Platforms must report within specific timeframes, creating 'overblocking' to avoid €50M fines. Content includes not just hate speech but 'incitement,' 'public disorder,' and 'economic sabotage' - categories applicable to union organizing and protest coordination. BKA performs 'initial legal check' without judicial oversight before referring to state prosecutors. Partner portals (HessenGegenHetze, REspect!, state media authorities) funnel complaints to federal police, creating state-funded informant ecosystem. Worker organizing communications on Facebook, Instagram, X subject to automated scanning and police reporting. Historical context: East German Stasi legacy makes German left particularly sensitive to state surveillance of speech. NetzDG amended 2024 to accommodate EU DSA but retained BKA reporting. Civil liberties groups (GFF, CCC) warn of 'speech policing within criminal justice machinery' and chilling effect on political discourse.
